name: Rhodium Laser Cleaning Dataset
description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for Rhodium. Includes thermal, optical, mechanical, and laser interaction properties validated against industry standards.

license.name: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International
license.description: Free to share and adapt with attribution

measurementTechnique: Laser ablation testing, material characterization, spectroscopy

includedInDataCatalog.name: Z-Beam Material Properties Database
includedInDataCatalog.description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for industrial applications

powerRange.description: Optimal average power for Rhodium surface cleaning without thermal damage

wavelength.description: Near-IR wavelength optimized for Rhodium's moderate reflectivity and thermal absorption

spotSize.description: Optimal beam diameter for precision cleaning resolution

repetitionRate.description: High repetition rate for efficient cleaning coverage

fluenceThreshold.description: Minimum fluence required for effective contaminant removal

pulseWidth.description: Nanosecond pulse duration for controlled ablation of contaminants

scanSpeed.description: Scanning speed for efficient area coverage

passCount.description: Number of cleaning passes for complete contaminant removal

overlapRatio.description: Optimal beam overlap for uniform cleaning results

power.description: Typical range for industrial Nd:YAG laser cleaners on reflective metals like Rhodium to balance efficiency and avoid thermal damage; recommended starting at 150W for contamination removal. Rhodium's high reflectivity (~65% at 1064 nm) requires higher power than less reflective substrates. Source: IPG Photonics YLR series specs and studies on PGM cleaning (e.g., Opt. Lasers Eng. 2018).

scan_speed.description: Effective speeds for 1064 nm cleaning of metals; slower for heavy contamination (500-1000 mm/s), faster for light (3000-5000 mm/s). Recommended 2000 mm/s for Rhodium to ensure contaminant removal without substrate heating, considering its thermal conductivity (150 W/m·K). Source: Industry standards from Laserax equipment manuals and process optimization in Surf. Coat. Technol. 2020.

pulse_width.description: Nanosecond regime preferred for cost-effective cleaning of Rhodium; picosecond (ps) or femtosecond for ultra-minimal HAZ but less common industrially. 20 ns recommended to minimize heat-affected zone on high-melting-point Rhodium (1964°C). Avoid fs for industrial scale due to complexity. Source: Laser physics research in Appl. Phys. A 2019 on metal ablation thresholds.

repetition_rate.description: Typical for pulsed cleaning to achieve high throughput; higher rates (50-100 kHz) speed up process but risk heat accumulation on reflective Rhodium. 50 kHz balances speed and safety. Trade-off: low freq reduces overlap heating. Source: Academic literature on Nd:YAG cleaning (e.g., J. Laser Appl. 2021).

energy_density.description: Fluence below Rhodium's ablation threshold (~4-6 J/cm² at 1064 nm due to high reflectivity) for safe contaminant removal. Recommended 1.5 J/cm² for light industrial cleaning; higher for heavy contamination risks damage. Conservative estimate based on similar PGMs. Source: Ablation threshold studies in Opt. Express 2017 and Appl. Surf. Sci. 2022.

spot_size.description: Smaller spots (50-200 μm) for fine surface preparation on Rhodium; larger (200-500 μm) for broad areas. 200 μm recommended for uniform cleaning without excessive overlap heating. Suited to Rhodium's use in plating/electronics. Source: Equipment specs from Coherent and Trumpf industrial lasers.

pass_count.description: Single pass for light contamination; multi-pass (2-5) for heavy or uneven layers on Rhodium. Recommended 2 passes for complete removal while preserving substrate integrity. Based on industrial practice for precious metals. Source: Case studies in Ind. Laser Sol. 2019 and process reports from Adapt Laser Systems.

overlap_ratio.description: Ensures uniform coverage without gaps or excessive energy input; 60% recommended for Rhodium to account for beam divergence and reflectivity, preventing uneven cleaning. Typical range from process optimization. Source: Studies in Laser Ablation and Surf. Eng. (Proc. SPIE 2020).
